segments.text: What is up, Coordination? On the pod today, we are talking about an AI DAO simulator.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: I feel like it's the ultimate nerd snipe. This is one of the coolest episodes that we've done.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: My guest today is James Pollack, who is an artist and programmer, has worked for various startups
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: and agencies building software and experiences in the past. We connected because of his interest
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: in complex systems and how agent-based modeling and DAOs could intersect. So basically at a high
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: level, this episode is about a DAO governance simulator that James has built. Basically,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: what if you took 100 different AIs and you gave them tokens and you gave them a governance forum
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: to argue in? What kind of things would you learn about DAO governance from that? So really
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: interesting design space that connects a lot of the recent trends around LLMs and artificial
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: intelligence with the things that we breathe every day, which is DAOs, Web3, decentralized
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: governance.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And protocols. So in this space, we often talk about how we're speed running the history of
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: coordination and of finance, but we can speed run it even faster if we set forward a bunch of AIs
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: to go argue in our governance forums and find the optimal parameters for DAO coordination so that we
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: don't have to do it ourselves. So really interesting design space. And James and I have been connected
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: for three to six months, sort of nerding out on these ideas. And James is actually building a code
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: base that's helping to explore some of these ideas. So coordination, I think this is a really fun
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: episode that combines some of the latest thinking on AI and DAOs. And if we are successful, we will
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: be speed running the design space of what's possible in DAO coordination using AIs, which,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: wow, the future is here. It's just so cool. So coordination, without further ado, I give you
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: James Pollack. Enjoy.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: ValleyDAO is an open global collective working to finance and democratize the governance of
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: synthetic biology technologies.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: To protect the future of our planet, research and progress into synthetic biology is critically
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: constrained by the world of nation-state regulations and bureaucracy from middlemen.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: But the potential that synthetic biology has for the world is massive. It can produce a world
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: in total harmony with nature. We can grow stuff using cells like software. It's awesome. And the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: best part is we no longer have to pull carbon out of the ground to grow and maintain civilization.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Don't you think we should be funding this? ValleyDAO does. And that's what they're here to do,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: to try and make the solar punk world
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of tomorrow a reality today. So join ValleyDAO right now and save the planet at ValleyDAO.bio.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: What's up, James?
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Hey, how are you, Kevin?
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: I'm pretty good. I'm excited to get weird. You ready to get weird?
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Yeah, same. I've been looking forward to our conversation since we first met a couple months
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: ago.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Yeah, same. So basically, maybe a good jumping off point would be for you to tell us what you've
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: been building this DAO simulator.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Sure.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So I've been working on an agent-based model. And an agent-based model is a computer program
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: where you can code a bunch of, I don't want to use the word in the definition. So instead of
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: agents, I'll say like characters. You can think of them like characters in a video game.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And you give them roles and rules of how they should behave. And then you run time through
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: the simulation. So there's a bunch of time steps. And every step, these agents make different
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: based on the rules that you've given them. And the situation that I've decided to put these
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: characters in is that they're all members of a decentralized autonomous organization. So the DAO
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: members are represented by different agent classes. And they can create proposals, vote on
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: them, invest in projects, a bunch of other activities. And the simulation also incorporates
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: treasury management, token pricing, revenue distribution, kind of things that happen in the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: happen at the level of the DAO itself, which is one agent, and then at the level of the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: individual DAO members who are your investors and delegators and developers.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: I just think it's so cool and I can't wait to unfurl the possibility space here.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So I think that the reason that you and I got connected was that on February 2nd, I
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: wrote this post on the Supermodular Community Forum about a DAO AI governance simulation
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: toolkit, which I called DAGs, just to try to meme it.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: But basically what I wrote was we could build an AI DAO toolkit where I could spin up a
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: hundred different AIs, give them all tokens and set each AI to maximize their token balances,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: give them a governance forum to argue in and let them vote on snapshot and tally.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And I want to run 10,000 different AI DAOs with slightly different starting conditions
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: and parameters for the agents, like they're friendly or adversarial, and see how those
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: DAOs evolve differently.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So basically we're doing a parameter sweep on governance frameworks.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And then you can start to introduce different rules to governance, or maybe you introduce
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: quadratic funding to the DAO, and you see how that affects the agent's behavior.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And once you've done this sweep of different setups, you could find optimal governance
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: setups across the design space of governance.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And to me, that's the exciting idea here is that instead of my friends having to argue
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: in the governance forum.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: In their DAO, what if we could have a bunch of AI's simulate that, and then find the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: optimal governance setups in for our human DAOs, actually set up our human DAOs that way.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So I wrote this post, and you and I got connected, and just sort of nerded out from there.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And I guess that's A, to give the reader the backstory of how we got connected, but B,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: just this design space feels like it's so exciting to me.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And I'd be curious maybe to...
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Alright, back to you. What got you excited about this design space? I think I had almost the exact
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: same realization as you, which is that there's actually very little data about this space and
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: that generating it takes time and money in real people's lives. And running experiments on actual
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: people in a DAO can be very painful for those people. And discovering whether a certain kind
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of voting system or penalties or rewards for behaving different ways inside of the DAO or
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: its structure itself, how many members, whether you have a native token, these are things that
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: are not easily changed, right? So if you want to test how your DAO would run with its own token or
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: not, that's quite hard to do.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So I also think that this opportunity to explore that design space, I love that you called it a
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: hyper parameter sweep, the technical term in some of the Python I'm using called batch running.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: But absolutely same thing. You want to see in the matrix of this choice and this choice that
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: you could make, how does it work when you choose A and B? How does it work when you choose A and C?
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: So on and so forth. And I think that maybe a little bit unlike you,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: I'm not so certain that we will be able to find those perfect platonic ideals of what a DAO should
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: be. I think that a lot of that will depend on what your particular goals are. And that's because
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: the agent-based models are typically, they're a source for generating synthetic data. So we're
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: modeling a system. It's not the real system. It's not going to, you know, it's not going to be a
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: necessarily have predictive properties, but that is sort of a really fun thing to work on is like,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: where do you put the slider as far as how synthetic is our data? You mentioned perhaps
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: letting your agents discuss things on a forum. Well, do you model that forum to just be a simple,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: like somebody left a positive, negative comment? Do you maybe build it out more fully so that the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: agents can talk to each other with LLMs? Or do you actually plug it into a real forum?
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And let your agents make posts and interact with real people? I think what you decide to do for that
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: will depend on what your goals are. But it's just exciting to be able to change those things. I've
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: seen DAOs try to do these and people make these big decisions with, you know, hundreds of millions
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of dollars under management with almost no insight into it. So I'm just hoping, you know, even if a
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: tool like this isn't entirely,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: predictive, I'm hoping that it'll at least give people some insight and help them have a better
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: decision making. Yeah, yeah, totally. I mean, I can imagine a future in which, you know, say I'm
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: on the governance or the steward council at DAO X. I'm imagining a future in which this tool
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: is available and you can ingest the parameters of the setup of the DAO and maybe ingest the
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: forum posts that have been on the DAO.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: And that sets the starting conditions in the DAO governance framework to be localized to whatever
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: DAO X values and cultural norms and setup is. And maybe you could, instead of sweeping the entire
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: parameter space, you could sort of sweep the space around it and say, you know, what would happen if
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: we cut our proposal threshold in half or if we changed our culture such that people were 20%
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: friendly or we introduced quadratic funding or something like that. To me, that's where it gets
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: really interesting. And yeah, you know, maybe it's not completely predictive, but it is an
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: interesting way of simulating DAO governance changes before you just YOLO make them live.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Right. I think you bring up a great point that there could be kind of two different users or
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: different times somebody might be trying to use this. One is you've got a DAO, it exists, you're
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: thinking about making a change. In that case, like, yeah, it's very hard to change the ship if
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: it's already underway. But maybe people thinking about it, they're like, oh, yeah, I'm going to
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: set up a new DAO. I think that's a situation where your entire hyperparameter sweep would be
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: pretty interesting. Yeah, totally. So can you take us into what you built? So for the listeners,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: if you click into the GitHub, you can see this code that James has put together around the DAO
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: simulation framework. And I'm looking at the readme now. So take us on a deep dive of what you built.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: For sure. So agent-based models,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: you can build in a variety of frameworks. The one that I had used previously was called NetLogo.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: That's a big one people learn when they are starting agent-based modelings. But it's based on
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: the JVM on Java. So it's not as, like, interoperable as you'd like. So for this project,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: I use a different agent-based modeling framework. This one's based in Python. It's called Mesa,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: which is great. You want to be in Python. That's where all the cool
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: stuff and generative things are happening. So being able to be in Python and plug in is super
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: exciting. So I've built agent-based models before. But maybe not one quite this big or complex. So
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: started with just sort of a base class of, like, a DAO member. You know, what does everybody need
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: to do? They vote. They comment on proposals. They hold tokens. And they have a reputation.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: Even some of these base assumptions,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: like, you're already making choices for the DAO, right? There's a reputation. We're keeping track
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of it with points. Like, already I can think of a ton of different ways to keep track of reputation.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: It can be kind of paralyzing because you want to give people, you know, every option. But you do
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: just have to make a couple arbitrary choices to get started and then hopefully leave in some levers
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: you can come back and tweak that stuff later. And so from the DAO member, I've, you know,
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: thought about some other roles
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of people. And so, you know, I've done a lot of research on this. And I think it's really cool.
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: of people in dao's and the things they might do so we have um like developers who um you know
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: be brought in to like build and maintain projects um investors invest in proposals and projects
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: um i've even modeled like passive members people who like hold tokens um and they might vote but
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: they're not really like getting involved in projects and stuff and um the proposal creators
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: who create and submit proposals regulators ensure compliance with external regulations
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: and requirements service service providers that's similar to a developer but it might be
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: like an external developer or if you need something translated
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: and i also have validators uh arbitrators delegators um you know looking at this list
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: i think going back if i was starting over i look at this and i think to myself like people often
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: are like oh I'm a developer, I'm an arbitrator... but
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: maybe people's roles change and this model doesn't quite account for that yet or maybe
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: people hold multiple roles um so starting with it's got about 10 different classes
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: uh and all the roles are individual roles um it's uh once you start running the model it'll
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: be interesting because your developers are going to like develop
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: they're not going to be investing in things but i think in the real world somebody can be
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: both an investor and a developer so i see some potential for that growing out later um so if
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: go ahead oh sorry i mean to interrupt um i was going to say like in a way um we're getting into
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: some of the fundamental trade-offs when it comes to modeling complex systems of which all economies
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: and social systems are uh it's just it's really hard when you've got these agent-based systems
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: and they're intelligent agents
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: to make uh productive assumptions about who is what and in what ways they behave and um i think
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: that that's kind of one of the challenges here is having the right level of abstraction that's not
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: too reductionist uh with each of these and you know i know from my experience working with say
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: say gitcoin dow which you know i'm now disaffiliated from but i've watched it
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: from the outside is that you know some of the people who are services providers are
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: also proposal creators are also developers are also delegators and
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: you're you're almost like overloading a super class at that point with different behavior types
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: um so yeah just a meta commentary about complex systems there um and a totally valid one and
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: one that makes me realize it's important to point out that these agents sort of out of the box
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: they're not ai they have like rules they're responding to conditions you're saying
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: you know if um token price is more than x do this if it's less than x do do that um
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: so in that sense like out of the box they're not very intelligent what is great about being in
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: this environment and actually all of the cool complex system stuff happening lately is that
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: your agents can actually be plugged into these systems like
segments.speaker: Kevin Owocki

segments.text: generative ai for creating those behaviors you could generate you know 200 different behaviors
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: and plug those in and have the agents pick from those or you could plug in
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: reinforcement learning and give your agents a you know reward function and so they could actually
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: change their behaviors on that or um you know let them be self-reflexive and actually modify
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: their behavior while it's running this simulation is not there yet but that is why i built it in
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: python and so that we have the potential every time you call a step on an agent that's how it
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: works as a time step but when we step the developer we don't just have to have them
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: you know do the rules that we wrote at the beginning
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: beginning of the simulation, if we plugged in the ability for them to generate new strategies,
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: try them out, kill the ones that don't work, and ride with the ones that do, yeah, then
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: stuff is going to get weird.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Then we're going to see some stuff in that hyperparameter space that we didn't even know
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: existed because they're going to be creating new parameters as they go.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: But level of abstraction is a huge problem with any kind of simulation, and I don't have
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: a great answer other than on the one side you have maybe a game, and then on the other
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: side you have your real data.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I do...
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: No, go ahead.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I was going to say, one of my hunches about how Web3 and AI are complementary is that
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I broadly think that Web3 in a way is about heuristics, and the heuristic that we're all
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: traditionally trained on is my bank account balance.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I want my bank account balance to go up.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: But in Web3, we now have...
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I have an internet native heuristic, which is my token balance.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And then AIs are about heuristic maximization in a way, which is how do I make the most
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: intelligent strategy to maximize that heuristic?
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And so I applied to this project, the insight was if you give an LLM or an AI a token balance
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: and you just say, make number go up, either your own token balance or the token balance
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: against let's call it ETH or USDC or something like that.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Yep.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: That's a lot of interesting behaviors.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: But just as a way of creating the multi-agent system and moving towards more advanced reflexive
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: behaviors versus rule-based behaviors, there's a ladder up there in terms of realism.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: So yeah, just the insight there was heuristics versus heuristic maximization.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I'm kind of thinking that's increasingly how AI and Web3 fit together.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Do you think that the DAO itself could have a heuristic maximization?
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I don't know.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Do you think it's heuristic beyond token go up?
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Do you think that these organizations have clear enough missions or strong enough missions
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: that it is something you could embody in the actual rules?
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: For example, the DAO could be set to maximize the token balances or it could be set to try
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: to redistribute the most income to its members and not care about having its own balance
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: be high.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Or it could just try to survive.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Yeah.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: There's been a bunch of different sort of goals for the DAO itself.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: I wonder what you think.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Yeah.

segments.text: What does Balaji say in the Network State book is that all of these network states have

segments.text: their one commandment, which is the thing that they think is wrong with the world, that

segments.text: their network's trying to change.

segments.text: And the toy example he gives is people think that we should be eating keto, and so there'll

segments.text: be a keto network state.

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: The next agent I think we'll make is a forum.

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: forum based, text based, threaded conversations.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And so I don't know if we'll need to model all of everything, but we probably will need
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: a fairly robust model.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: be fun.

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: you model a social space for a digital...
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Just having been involved in DAOs, I think that the governance forum is where the formal
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: governance happens.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: But I learned this thing about organizations, oh man, like 15 years ago, one of my mentors
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: taught me that like, hey, the real culture of this is not within the meetings, it's in
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: the hallways and like in the happy hours.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And what people are doing.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: What people actually say to each other when they're not afraid of reprisals from the boss.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And that makes me think, oh, well, the DAOs can fight in the governance forum, but what
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: are they Discord DMing to each other and what are they tweeting about?
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: And so simulating those social spaces is, it seems complicated, but with LLMs, it actually
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: feels newly doable.

segments.text: Newly easier.
segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: Yeah.

segments.speaker: James Pollack

segments.text: quite as far as they have now.

segments.speaker: James Pollack
